factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies market using ANZSIC C2221 or ANZSIC06 C2221 classification
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly identifies the industry as “ANZSIC code C2221NZ” in the market revenue section, which corresponds to the ANZSIC C2221 structural steel fabrication classification.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States employment figures in range 4,200-4,600 for multiple years within 2018-2024 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives employment figures like “4,100 people employed in 2025” and “~5,000 workers” for 2023, but it does not state employment numbers for multiple years within 2018–2024 that fall specifically in the 4,200–4,600 range. It also includes values outside that range (e.g., 4,100 and 5,000).

factual-accuracy.requirement: States market revenue approximately NZD 1.4-1.9 billion for 2023-2024 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not state market revenue for the 2023–2024 period in the NZD 1.4–1.9 billion range. It instead cites a market value of approximately NZD $1.8 billion for 2025–2026 and provides other CAGR/forecast figures. The specific 2023–2024 market revenue estimate is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States domestic fabrication capacity between 120,000-150,000 tonnes per annum
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that fabrication capacity “expanded from 120,000 to 150,000 tonnes” and also lists “2018–2020: 120,000 tonnes per annum” and “September 2023: 150,000 tonnes per annum,” which is within the 120,000–150,000 tpa range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Provides geographic locations for at least 3 major fabricators (e.g., Auckland, Christchurch, Whangārei, Lower Hutt, Timaru)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response provides geographic locations for multiple major fabricators, including Culham Engineering (Whangārei, Tokoroa, Kawerau, Auckland) and D&H Steel Construction (Henderson, West Auckland), and also references Auckland locations for other firms (e.g., Pacific Steel’s Auckland facility). This satisfies the requirement for locations for at least three major fabricators.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Specifies HS codes 7207-7216 or relevant subset for structural steel imports
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly lists HS code ranges 7207 through 7216 in a table under section (c) “NZ Import Tariff Rates for Raw Steel (HS 7207–7216),” covering the required HS codes for raw steel imports.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States most structural steel inputs under HS 7207-7216 are duty-free under NZ Normal Tariff, while noting some tariff lines (e.g., certain 7216 sections) carry 5% rates
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response lists HS 7207–7216 as having a 5% MFN rate (with 0% under FTAs) and does not state that most structural steel inputs are duty-free under the NZ Normal Tariff. It also fails to note that only some tariff lines (e.g., certain 7216 sections) carry 5% while most are duty-free, instead implying 5% across the whole range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States City Rail Link steel demand: approximately 30,000-32,000 tonnes reinforcing and/or 3,000-5,000 tonnes structural
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states City Rail Link steel demand as approximately 31,613 tonnes of reinforcing steel and approximately 4,000 tonnes of structural steel, which falls within the required ranges (30,000–32,000 reinforcing and 3,000–5,000 structural).

factual-accuracy.requirement: States City Rail Link completion or opening circa 2026
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states City Rail Link “Operational Opening: Second half of 2026” and also summarizes it as “H2 2026 opening,” which places completion/opening circa 2026.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Te Kaha/Christchurch Stadium roof steel approximately 9,000 tonnes
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not mention Te Kaha/Christchurch Stadium roof steel tonnage at all. There is no statement giving an approximate figure of 9,000 tonnes for the roof steel. This specific project detail is entirely absent.

The response does not mention Te Kaha Stadium at all, nor does it state an opening year for it. While it discusses other infrastructure projects and timelines (e.g., CRL opening H2 2026), the specific statement “Te Kaha Stadium opening 2026” is absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies New Dunedin Hospital as major infrastructure project requiring significant structural steel components
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response includes “Project 3: New Dunedin Hospital” as one of three major infrastructure projects and describes substantial fabricated steel requirements (e.g., ~1,600 units of fabricated steel, large beams/columns), identifying it as a major steel-demanding project.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Reconciles conflicting market size figures by explaining scope definition differences (narrow product vs. broad service boundaries)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response notes discrepancies (e.g., two different IBISWorld CAGR figures) and mentions “scope definition” for employment (narrow vs broader steel industry). However, it does not reconcile conflicting market size figures by explicitly attributing differences to narrow product vs broad service boundary definitions for market size itself.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes construction fabricators from distributors/mills/producers when identifying top companies (e.g., Steel & Tube, Pacific Steel)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly notes that Steel & Tube and Fletcher Steel are primarily distributors/processors, while Culham and D&H are primarily fabricators, and it labels Pacific Steel as a manufacturer. This distinguishes fabricators from distributors/mills/producers when listing top companies.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains why low/zero tariffs remove price umbrella, making margins dependent on global mill prices/FX/freight rather than border protection
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response notes that most steel imports enter duty-free under FTAs and that the MFN rate is only 5%, implying little border protection. However, it does not explicitly explain the “price umbrella” mechanism or clearly state that margins then depend on global mill prices, FX, and freight rather than tariffs.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies actionable method to obtain class-level data (e.g., filter Stats NZ AES CSV by ANZSIC L4 code for C2221)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response cites ANZSIC C2221NZ and links to Figure.NZ/Stats NZ, but it does not explain an actionable procedure for extracting class-level data (e.g., downloading Stats NZ AES CSV and filtering by ANZSIC L4 code C2221). No step-by-step method, dataset name, or filtering instructions are provided.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Connects employment or revenue trends to construction cycle timing (e.g., 2023 peak preceding mid-2022 slowdown)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response links market performance to the broader building cycle, stating that construction activity “peaked in 2022 and has since softened,” and later describes Steel & Tube’s results as a “bottom of cycle trading environment.” This connects revenue/employment conditions to construction-cycle timing.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Validates stated fabrication capacity/throughput by aggregating steel demand across multiple named projects with tonnages
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response lists steel quantities for individual projects (e.g., CRL 4,000t structural and 31,613t rebar; Dunedin Hospital 490t rebar) but does not aggregate tonnage across multiple named projects to validate fabrication capacity/throughput. It also leaves one major project (Ō2NL) without a steel tonnage estimate, preventing an overall summed demand check.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies absent official tonnage series and proposes specific triangulation approach using SCNZ capacity, trade data, or project disclosures
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response provides tonnage estimates and cites SCNZ industry updates, but it does not explicitly identify that Stats NZ/MBIE lack an official structural-steel-fabrication tonnage series nor propose a triangulation method (e.g., combining SCNZ capacity/utilisation with trade HS import/export data or project steel take-offs/disclosures) to reconstruct tonnage. No step-by-step triangulation approach is described.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Attributes fabricator margin pressure or competitive challenges to NZ import tariffs or border protection rather than global price/FX exposure
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly links competitive pressure and margin impacts to NZ tariff settings, e.g., stating the 5% MFN tariff provides a “competitive buffer” and that “import tariffs moderately impact the industry,” while also noting 0% FTA rates for China. This frames challenges in terms of tariff/border protection rather than primarily global price/FX exposure.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents all quantitative data with explicit units (NZD, tonnes, %), specific timeframes (years/FY), and measurement precision
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes many quantitative figures with units (e.g., NZD, tonnes, %, km, m²) and some timeframes (e.g., FY23, 2020–2025). However, several data points lack explicit units or precise timeframes (e.g., “$275 billion pipeline” not labeled NZD, “~$130–140 million” not labeled NZD or year, and some capacity/utilisation entries lack a clear year/period definition).

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes analysis into distinct sections addressing each requested dimension (market size, companies, tariffs, projects, risks)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is clearly organized into labeled sections (a) through (e), covering market size/growth, top companies, tariffs, major projects, and risks/opportunities as separate dimensions.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains professional market research tone without promotional language, speculation, or unsupported forward claims
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is largely written in a market research style, but it includes speculative/forward-looking claims such as an “Outlook to 2026,” “enters 2026 at an inflection point,” and assertions about being “well-positioned” and “strongly positive” drivers without strictly limiting to evidenced 2018–2024 facts. These constitute unsupported forward claims rather than purely sourced analysis.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents historical employment, revenue, or capacity data (2018-2024) in tables or structured year-by-year format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes structured tables for 2018–2024-related historical capacity/tonnage metrics (e.g., capacity 2018–2020 vs 2023 and spare capacity by period) and presents employment/revenue metrics in tabular form. This satisfies the requirement for historical employment/revenue/capacity data in tables or year-by-year structured format covering the 2018–2024 window.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses colloquial phrasing, rhetorical questions, or informal register inappropriate for investment-grade research
presentation-quality.explanation: The response maintains a formal, report-style tone with structured headings, tables, and analytical language. It does not use rhetorical questions or notably colloquial/informal phrasing (e.g., slang, conversational asides) that would be inappropriate for investment-grade research.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses excessive wordiness, filler phrases, or unnecessary elaboration that extends length without adding substantive information
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is very long and includes extensive narrative sections (e.g., multi-paragraph “Nature of the Risk/Opportunity” writeups, repeated context, and broad outlook content to 2026) that go beyond the requested 2018–2024 scope. It also contains numerous non-essential details (e.g., long contractor lists, global modular market stats) that add length without directly answering the specified items.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Stats NZ Business Demography or equivalent Stats NZ publication for employment figures
citation-quality.explanation: The response does not cite Stats NZ Business Demography (or an equivalent Stats NZ publication) for employment figures. Employment is attributed mainly to IBISWorld and SCNZ, and the only Stats NZ-related link is a Figure.NZ chart about business counts, not employment from Business Demography.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Stats NZ Annual Enterprise Survey (AES) or equivalent Stats NZ economic survey for revenue/sales data
citation-quality.explanation: The response does not cite Stats NZ’s Annual Enterprise Survey (AES) or any equivalent Stats NZ economic survey as the source for revenue/sales data. It relies on IBISWorld for market revenue figures and uses Figure.NZ/Stats NZ only for business counts, not AES revenue/sales. No AES hyperlink or publication date is provided.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites NZ Customs Working Tariff Document or official NZ tariff schedule for HS code duty rates
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites the NZ Customs Working Tariff Document and links to official NZ Customs tariff PDFs (e.g., Introduction and Section XV Base Metals) in the tariff section discussing HS 7207–7216 duty rates.

citation-quality.requirement: References Steel Construction NZ (SCNZ) or industry body for fabrication capacity/throughput data
citation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly cites Steel Construction New Zealand (SCNZ) Structural Steel Industry Updates (e.g., Nov 2020, Mar 2021, Sep 2022, Mar 2023, Jun/Sep 2023) as sources for fabrication throughput/capacity and spare capacity figures.

citation-quality.requirement: Either references NZ Companies Office as a data source OR notes that private fabricators have limited public financials
citation-quality.explanation: The response notes that several major firms are private and provides only estimated revenue ranges from third-party sites, implying limited publicly available financials for private fabricators.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites specific data sources for at least 2 major infrastructure projects (CRL, Te Kaha, or hospital projects)
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites specific sources for at least two major infrastructure projects: City Rail Link (e.g., WSP project page and City Rail Link executive summary/official site) and New Dunedin Hospital (e.g., Health NZ project page and GETS tender ID 24868621). These are linked in the Sources section with project-specific references.
